title: Programming long-range interactions in analog quantum simulators
authors: Cristian Tabares, Alberto Muñoz de las Heras, Jan T. Schneider, Alejandro González-Tudela
abstract: Long-range interactions are the source of many equilibrium and out-of-equilibrium quantum many-body phenomena. Analog simulators based on ionic, atomic, superconducting, and molecular systems provide a natural platform to obtain these interactions using vibration- and photon-mediated processes. Recent experimental advances, such as their integration in multi-mode cavities and waveguides, or the use of Raman-assisted transitions, enable dynamical control over both the strength and the spatial range of these interactions, thereby rendering them programmable. Here, we develop a hybrid classical-quantum toolbox that exploits this tunability to enhance many-body state preparation in analog simulators beyond fixed-connectivity architectures. Our approach is based on classical pre-compilation in homogeneous small systems, whose optimized parameters are extrapolated iteratively to larger system sizes, and then refined on the quantum hardware using noise-aware hybrid re-optimization and error-mitigation techniques. We benchmark this strategy across several fermionic, spin-1/2, and spin-1 models, demonstrating orders-of-magnitude improvements in fidelity and energy estimation for system sizes ranging from 100 to 1000 particles. Finally, we show that the combination of such high-fidelity programmable state preparation techniques with tunable-range out-of-equilibrium dynamics enables controlled studies of many-body thermalization in regimes accessible to current experimental platforms. Our results establish programmable long-range interactions as a powerful resource for next-generation analog quantum simulators.
summary: This paper introduces a method to leverage programmable long-range interactions in analog quantum simulators to enhance many-body state preparation. By using a hybrid approach of classical pre-compilation and quantum re-optimization, the authors show that long-range connectivity allows for much higher fidelity and lower energy error compared to standard nearest-neighbor models. The strategy scales effectively to systems of up to 1000 particles and can be used to study complex phenomena like many-body thermalization. This approach turns interaction range into a powerful tool for next-generation quantum simulation.
main_problem: Analog quantum simulators are often limited by fixed-connectivity architectures and limited Hamiltonian tunability, which hinders efficient many-body state preparation and the study of complex dynamics.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that programmable long-range (PR) interactions significantly outperform nearest-neighbor architectures, providing orders-of-magnitude improvements in fidelity and energy estimation for systems up to 1000 particles.
method: A hybrid classical-quantum toolbox using classical pre-compilation on small systems, iterative extrapolation to larger sizes, and quantum hardware-based re-optimization combined with Zero-Noise Extrapolation (ZNE).
model_or_system: The study utilizes programmable-range Hamiltonians (exponential and power-law decay) applied to various models including the Transverse Field Ising Model (TFIM), Spin-1 Blume-Capel, Kitaev chains, and the Aubry-André-Harper (AAH) model, relevant to platforms like trapped ions, Rydberg atoms, and waveguide/cavity QED.
key_observables: Residual energy density, infidelity density, energy estimation, and many-body thermalization signatures (e.g., site occupation dynamics).
important_parameters: Interaction strength (J), spatial range (L), circuit depth (D), training size (N1), target system size (N2), and noise strength (gamma).
main_assumptions: The work focuses on 1D models and assumes the scalability of the method to higher dimensions remains a subject for future testing; it also relies on the classical simulability of quadratic models for large-scale benchmarking.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the PR Hamiltonian resource; Figure 2 compares residual energy and infidelity for PR vs. NN architectures and demonstrates scalability; Figure 3 illustrates the ZNE and error-mitigation pipeline; Figure 4 shows Kitaev chain scaling and optimized control pulses; Figure 5 compares performance in the Spin-1 BC model; Figure 6 demonstrates thermalization dynamics under PR vs. NN quenches.
paper_structure: The paper defines the programmable-range resource, describes the hybrid optimization and pre-compilation toolbox, benchmarks scalability and performance across various spin and fermionic models, and concludes by demonstrating applications in studying many-body thermalization.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful framework for using tunable long-range interactions as a control resource to break integrability, study thermalization, and achieve high-fidelity state preparation in large-scale analog simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Quantum resource: evolution under programmable-range (PR) Hamiltonians with on-site drivings HL(h) and tunable-range interactions HPR(J, L), whose spatial profile is set by fL(|i −j|). These operations define a gate set G used to construct parametrized quantum circuits. (b) Classical resource: pre- and post-processing techniques to calculate the optimal pulses to be implemented in the simulator using the hybrid workflow combining classical and quantum resources described in panels (c-e).
